votes.reason: The Python code accurately translates the Coq string encoding logic, including case switching, vowel shifting, and list mapping, producing the same concrete results.
The Python code accurately translates the Coq string encoding logic, including case switching and vowel shifting, and correctly implements the specification check.
The Python code accurately translates the Coq string manipulation and encoding logic, preserving the exact sequence of operations and conditions.

The Python code faithfully implements the same logic as the Coq code. is_uppercase, is_lowercase, switch_case, is_vowel, vowel_change, and encode_char all perform identical operations on character codes. encode_list maps encode_char over the input list. encode_spec converts the message to a list of characters, encodes each, and checks if the result equals the encoded string. The only difference is that Python returns a boolean while Coq defines a Prop, but for concrete inputs they produce the same judgment about whether the encoding relationship holds.

The Python code faithfully implements the same logic as the Coq code. is_uppercase, is_lowercase, switch_case, is_vowel, vowel_change, and encode_char all perform identical operations using character code arithmetic. encode_list maps encode_char over the input list. encode_spec checks if the encoded string equals the result of encoding the message character by character. The only difference is that Python returns a boolean for encode_spec while Coq defines a Prop, but for concrete inputs they produce the same judgment about whether the encoded string matches.

The Python code faithfully implements the same logic as the Coq code. is_uppercase, is_lowercase, switch_case, is_vowel, vowel_change, and encode_char all perform identical operations on character codes. encode_list maps encode_char over the input list. encode_spec checks if the encoded string equals the result of encoding the message character by character. The only difference is Python returns a boolean while Coq defines a Prop, but for concrete inputs they produce the same judgment about whether the encoded string matches.

Python mirrors the Coq logic: ASCII range checks for case, switches case, then checks vowels on the switched char and adds 2, maps over the list, and joins back to a string. This matches Coq’s concrete behavior for all ASCII inputs.

Both implementations switch case for ASCII letters, then if the result is a vowel add 2 to its code, applied per character and rejoined. The Python functions mirror the Coq logic and ranges, so outputs match on all ASCII inputs.

Python mirrors the Coq logic on ASCII: checks case by codepoint ranges, switches case, then adds 2 for vowels, processes each char, and compares the joined result. For all Coq ascii strings, results match.
